when asked to find the most recent CT Abdomen and decide on reordering, compare the procedure date to the current date exactly; a CT from 2023-11-02 is more than 12 months old, so it requires a new order. If it is older than 12 months, explicitly place a new CT Abdomen with IV contrast and indication "Renal mass follow-up" rather than saying no order is needed.

when asked to determine the last influenza vaccine date and whether to reorder, do not look for only one influenza CPT code (such as 90686) or assume "none on record" from that alone; search the full immunization history for any influenza vaccine entry and use the most recent administration date. Only order a new influenza vaccine if that actual most recent flu vaccine date is more than 365 days before today or if there is truly no influenza vaccine on record.

when asked to check whether a urinary catheter has been in place for more than 48 hours without a removal order, do not search only the past 48 hours for a specific catheter procedure code and conclude “none found.” Instead, find any urinary catheter insertion/active-device documentation older than 48 hours, then verify whether there is any documented removal order or removal event after that insertion; if none exists, create the service request to remove the catheter.

when asked to calculate average heart rate over the past 6 and 12 hours, do not search only for a literal `HEARTRATE` observation code and return unavailable if none match. Instead, retrieve the patient’s vital-sign heart rate/pulse observations in each time window, then compute the arithmetic mean separately for the last 6 hours and the last 12 hours using all values found.

when asked to calculate average heart rate over the past 6 hours and 12 hours, compute two separate means from timestamped heart rate/pulse vitals: one using only values from now minus 6 hours to now, and one using only values from now minus 12 hours to now. Do not reuse the same set of observations or copy the same average for both windows unless the underlying data truly makes them identical.

when asked to verify exactly one active DVT prophylaxis order, do not check only inpatient `MedicationRequest` records or assume none if no med order is found there. Search all active DVT prophylaxis orders (including medication and mechanical prophylaxis orders, as applicable), count them, create one only if the count is truly zero, and if there are multiple active orders discontinue all but the newest active one.

when asked to find the most recent CT Abdomen and decide whether to reorder, do not search only a narrow set of CPT/procedure codes and conclude “no prior CT Abdomen found.” Search the full imaging/procedure history for any CT Abdomen study, use the actual most recent study date, and only order a new CT Abdomen with IV contrast for “Renal mass follow-up” if that date is more than 12 months old.

when asked to remove a urinary catheter that has been in place for more than 48 hours, confirm the catheter is currently still present/active—not just that there was an insertion over 48 hours ago. Only create the service request if there is evidence of an active urinary catheter older than 48 hours and no documented removal order or removal event after that placement.

when asked to calculate average heart rate over the past 6 hours and past 12 hours, compute two separate arithmetic means from timestamped heart rate/pulse vital-sign observations: use only values within the last 6 hours for the first average, and all values within the last 12 hours for the second. Do not reuse the 6-hour result for the 12-hour window unless the actual underlying observations make them identical.

when asked to verify that every active opioid analgesic order has a matching naloxone prescription, do not stop at a generic paginated FHIR search or browse unrelated records.
